aiken/interval/IntervalBound.title: IntervalBound
aiken/interval/IntervalBound.description: An interval bound, either inclusive or exclusive.

aiken/interval/IntervalBoundType.title: IntervalBoundType
aiken/interval/IntervalBoundType.description:
A type of interval bound. The value for the `Finite` case typically
 represents a number of seconds or milliseconds.

aiken/merkle_patricia_forestry/Neighbor.title: Neighbor
aiken/merkle_patricia_forestry/Neighbor.description: since | 1.0.0 --- | --- A neighbor node used in a proof. See [Proof](#Proof) for details.

aiken/merkle_patricia_forestry/ProofStep.title: ProofStep
aiken/merkle_patricia_forestry/ProofStep.description: since | 1.0.0 --- | --- We distinguish three kind of proof steps: Branch, Fork and Leaf. Each step contains a `skip` value which corresponds to the length of the common prefix at that particular level. The details of each level is documented [in the wiki :: Proof Format](https://github.com/aiken-lang/merkle-patricia-forestry/wiki/Proof-format).

aiken_design_patterns/linked_list/Elemen.title: ElementData
aiken_design_patterns/linked_list/Elemen.description:
Similar to [`Element`](#Element) above, the API is designed so that you
 wouldn't need to work with this datatype directly.

 Continuations that provide element data implicitly signal whether it should
 be coerced into a `root_data` or a `node_data` through the (potential) key.
 That is, if no key is provided, it means the element at hand is a root
 element.

aiken_design_patterns/validity_range_nor.title: NormalizedTimeRange
aiken_design_patterns/validity_range_nor.description:
Datatype for eliminating meaningless ranges, without the redundant
 inclusiveness flag (instead all range values are inclusive).

cardano/address/Address.title: Address
cardano/address/Address.description:
A Cardano `Address` typically holding one or two credential references.

 Note that legacy bootstrap addresses (a.k.a. 'Byron addresses') are
 completely excluded from Plutus contexts. Thus, from an on-chain
 perspective only exists addresses of type 00, 01, ..., 07 as detailed
 in [CIP-0019 :: Shelley Addresses](https://github.com/cardano-foundation/CIPs/tree/master/CIP-0019/#shelley-addresses).

cardano/address/Credential.title: Credential
cardano/address/Credential.description:
A general structure for representing an on-chain `Credential`.

 Credentials are always one of two kinds: a direct public/private key
 pair, or a script (native or Plutus).

cardano/address/PaymentCredential.title: PaymentCredential
cardano/address/PaymentCredential.description:
A general structure for representing an on-chain `Credential`.

 Credentials are always one of two kinds: a direct public/private key
 pair, or a script (native or Plutus).

cardano/address/StakeCredential.title: StakeCredential
cardano/address/StakeCredential.description:
Represent a type of object that can be represented either inline (by hash)
 or via a reference (i.e. a pointer to an on-chain location).

 This is mainly use for capturing pointers to a stake credential
 registration certificate in the case of so-called pointer addresses.

anyOf.title: DatumHash
anyOf.description: A datum referenced by its hash digest.

anyOf.title: InlineDatum
anyOf.description: A datum completely inlined in the output.

cardano/transaction/OutputReference.title: OutputReference
cardano/transaction/OutputReference.description:
An `OutputReference` is a unique reference to an output on-chain. The `output_index`
 corresponds to the position in the output list of the transaction (identified by its id)
 that produced that output

cardano/transaction/ValidityRange.title: ValidityRange

midgard/ledger_state/DepositId.title: DepositId
midgard/ledger_state/DepositId.description:
An `OutputReference` is a unique reference to an output on-chain. The `output_index`
 corresponds to the position in the output list of the transaction (identified by its id)
 that produced that output

midgard/ledger_state/MidgardAddress.title: MidgardAddress
midgard/ledger_state/MidgardAddress.description:
A general structure for representing an on-chain `Credential`.

 Credentials are always one of two kinds: a direct public/private key
 pair, or a script (native or Plutus).

midgard/ledger_state/MidgardInputId.title: MidgardInputId
midgard/ledger_state/MidgardInputId.description: Datatype to allow inputs of a Midgard tx to be in custom order.

midgard/ledger_state/MidgardNetworkId.title: MidgardNetworkId
midgard/ledger_state/MidgardNetworkId.description:
Midgard network ID
 It indicates the network for which the transaction is intended.
 Compact representation.

midgard/ledger_state/MidgardScript.title: MidgardScript
midgard/ledger_state/MidgardScript.description:
Midgard script -- either timelock (native) or Plutus (V3).
 Full representation.

midgard/ledger_state/TxOrderId.title: TxOrderId
midgard/ledger_state/TxOrderId.description:
An `OutputReference` is a unique reference to an output on-chain. The `output_index`
 corresponds to the position in the output list of the transaction (identified by its id)
 that produced that output

midgard/ledger_state/WithdrawalId.title: WithdrawalId
midgard/ledger_state/WithdrawalId.description:
An `OutputReference` is a unique reference to an output on-chain. The `output_index`
 corresponds to the position in the output list of the transaction (identified by its id)
 that produced that output

midgard/operator_directory/active_operat.title: Datum
midgard/operator_directory/active_operat.description:
The terminology used here is as follows:
 - `Root` is the first element of the linked list
 - `Node` is any other element in a linked list other than the root
 - `Element` is the umbrella term to refer to either the root or nodes

 You probably won't need to interact with this.

midgard/operator_directory/active_operat.title: MintRedeemer
midgard/operator_directory/active_operat.description:
TODO: Add a "slash duplicate operator" constructor as well. Currently only
       the registered operators set has this endpoint, where it can slash
       operators who are on the registered set, and also on any of the other
       sets (including registered set itself, as its node keys are not PKHs).
       However, since this slashing is not _mandatory_, it is _possible_ (but
       highly unlikely) for a duplicate operator to end up in the active set
       and the retired set (e.g. an active operator retires, registers anew,
       doesn't get slashed for duplication, and finally activates). This
       can lock the scheduler for the duration of an entire shift: if the
       duplicate operator neglects their duties, they will receive inactivity
       strikes until its max is reached, which renders them eligible for
       partial slashing and transfer to the retired set. But since there
       exists a duplicate node there, the transfer transaction cannot go
       through. When the scheduler rewinds and appoints the duplicate
       operator again, the operator cannot be skipped as its strike count
       can't be incremented further.

       Adding this constructor to the retired operators set might be better
       as it won't require involvement of the scheduler UTxO.
